data:
9th Grade. The entry point into full participation in the life of the church.
Let’s be very clear about what Confirmation is — and is not:
It is not graduation from church. It is not a gold star for attending. It is not a hoop to jump through so parents get the photo. It is three things, all at once:
God confirms a youth’s baptismal identity as a beloved child of God. The youth confirms their faith in Jesus Christ — not their parents’ faith, not their grandmother’s faith, their own. Everyone confirms that this is the beginning, not the end. Confirmation works best when it’s relational, not informational. A student will forget most of the doctrine by Thanksgiving. They won’t forget the adult who knew their name, asked their hard questions, and prayed for them by name on Confirmation Sunday.
data.series#Series: 🛠️ Still under construction. A full Confirmation curriculum is coming. In the meantime, if your church has a framework that works — the Apostles’ Creed, the Westminster Shorter Catechism, the Heidelberg, or something homegrown — please contribute it.
data.whats-coming#What\u0026rsquo;s coming:
Planned curriculum:
Who Is God? — The Trinity without the word salad Who Is Jesus? — Looking squarely at the claims, the cross, and the resurrection Who Am I? — Identity as a baptized child of God What Do We Do Now? — Worship, Word, sacraments, and service as a way of life The Credo Project — each student writes their own “I believe” statement to share on Confirmation Sunday

data:
Grades 9–12. The ages that ask the hardest questions, barter for compliance, and will spot spiritual BS at 400 yards. So we try not to serve any.
HS lessons on Opensource Sunday are built for students who are done being entertained and ready to be engaged. The template is the same as Middle School — Behind the Curtain → Center Stage → Prop Department → Directing the Scene — but with heavier discussion prompts, more Scripture per session, and fewer snack-based metaphors (though the snacks themselves remain non-negotiable).
data.series#Series: 🚧 Nothing to show here yet. The HS curriculum is still being drafted. If you’ve got a series you want to contribute — or a lesson you’ve already taught that killed — open a PR.
data.whats-coming#What\u0026rsquo;s coming:
Planned series include:
Romans Road Trip — an 8-week walk through Paul’s letter to Rome Hard Sayings of Jesus — 6 weeks on the verses nobody puts on a coffee mug The Psalms, for People Who Aren’t Fine — 5 weeks in the raw honesty of the Psalms Apologetics Without the Argument — 4 weeks on doubt, evidence, and faith Stay tuned. Or, better yet, write one yourself.

data:
Grades 6–8. The ages most adults pretend they forgot. The ages that, according to the Barna Group, are when nearly 64% of lifelong Christians first made a commitment to Christ. Coincidence? Probably not.
These lessons are written for the real middle schooler — the one who laughs too loud, then goes quiet when it matters, then asks a question that’ll make you rethink your whole theology on the car ride home.
data.series#Series: Sermon on the MountNine weeks in Matthew 5–7. A new way to be human.
data.what-to-expect-from-an-ms-lesson#What to:
Every lesson on this site follows the same template so volunteers aren’t left guessing:
Behind the Curtain — what the teacher needs to know before walking in Center Stage — the ONE thing you want students to walk out with Prop Department — the shopping list Directing the Scene — the actual plan: prayer, hook, Scripture, discussion, and a tangible “turn toward action” All MS lessons target ~45–60 minutes and are written assuming a group of 6–15 students, family-style. Scale the activities to your room.

data: A nine-week series through the Sermon on the Mount. Scripture anchor: Matthew 5–7 Series ONE thing: Jesus doesn’t just want us to follow the rules harder — he’s inviting us into a brand new way to be human, built on him.
data.jump-to-a-lesson#Jump to a lesson: Lesson 1 · Up the MountainMatthew 5:1–12 · The Beatitudes — who gets called blessed? Lesson 2 · Salt and LightMatthew 5:13–16 · Flavor and illumination Lesson 3 · But I SayMatthew 5:17–48 · Heart, not just behavior draft Lesson 4 · When You PrayMatthew 6:5–15 · Talk to your Father draft Lesson 5 · Treasures & Two MastersMatthew 6:19–24 · What has your heart has your life draft Lesson 6 · Do Not WorryMatthew 6:25–34 · Trust the One who feeds sparrows draft Lesson 7 · Judge NotMatthew 7:1–6 · Check the plank before the speck draft Lesson 8 · Ask, Seek, KnockMatthew 7:7–12 · A good Father and the Golden Rule draft Lesson 9 · House on the RockMatthew 7:24–29 · Hear and do. Build on Jesus. draft Leading this series? The nine lessons are designed as one arc — identity first, then witness, heart, prayer, treasure, trust, judgment, asking, and finally foundation. Lesson 9 builds an explicit series recap into the teaching, so the ordering matters. If you only have time for a shorter arc, at minimum do Lessons 1 and 9 together.

data: Scripture: Matthew 5:1–12 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
By the end of Matthew 4, Jesus has become the most talked-about rabbi in the region. He’s been traveling all over Galilee, teaching in synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom, and healing every kind of sickness and pain. Crowds from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan are chasing him down. Then, in Matthew 5:1, he sees the crowd, walks up a mountainside, sits down (the posture of a teacher in that day), and begins the most famous sermon ever preached.
Before Jesus says a single word about how to live, he tells us who God is cheering for. The “Beatitudes” (from the Latin beatus — blessed, happy, flourishing) are not a checklist of behaviors to unlock God’s favor. They are Jesus announcing that God’s kingdom runs on an upside-down scoreboard. The people the world calls losers — the poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek, the hungry, the merciful, the pure in heart, the peacemakers, the persecuted — God calls blessed. That would have sounded as scandalous then as it does now.
For middle schoolers, this is enormous. They live in a world where “blessed” almost always means rich, pretty, followed, talented, winning. Jesus just took a sharpie to that whole list.
Two patterns worth pointing out:
Every Beatitude has the same shape: Blessed are ___, for they will ___. A condition, then a promise. The bookends match: verses 3 and 10 both end “for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.” Jesus wraps the whole list in the same promise — this isn’t a waiting game. The kingdom is already here for these people. This lesson is the first of two in the Sermon on the Mount series. Next week we fly through the rest of the sermon (Matt 5:13–7:27). Tonight we stay parked on the Beatitudes.
data.catch-their-attention--the-scoreboard#Ca:
Before the lesson: Write across the top of the whiteboard in big letters: “WHO IS BLESSED?”
During the lesson: Say:
Raise your hand if you’ve ever heard someone say “I’m blessed” — on a post, in a song, on a jersey, anywhere. When people say that, what do they usually mean?
Let students shout out answers. Write them on the board in one color. (You’ll probably get: rich, healthy, popular, good family, winning, famous, lots of followers, talented, pretty, etc.)
Then say:
Okay — same question, but now in Jesus’ version. If Jesus wrote the list, who do you think he’d put on it?
Don’t give the answer yet. Let them guess. Write their guesses in a different color. Then say:
Hold onto that. Let’s read what Jesus actually says — and see how close we got.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
God’s kingdom runs on an upside-down scoreboard. The people the world overlooks are the ones Jesus calls “blessed.”
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Looking back at our board — how different is Jesus’ list from what the world calls “blessed”? Which of the eight Beatitudes hits you the hardest right now? Not the easiest. The one you need the most. Who in your school do most people ignore or laugh at — who Jesus might actually be calling “blessed”? Have you ever felt like one of the people on Jesus’ list? (Poor in spirit, grieving, small, overlooked?) What would it mean to you to hear Jesus call you “blessed” right there? Why do you think Jesus started his biggest sermon with this list? What is he trying to do to us before he teaches us anything else?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student Whiteboard or large paper + markers (two colors) Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening) Optional: a printed card for each student with all 8 Beatitudes to take home
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-5112#Read an:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Over the next two weeks we’re going to be in the most famous sermon Jesus ever preached — something called the Sermon on the Mount. Three whole chapters of Jesus teaching us how to live in God’s kingdom. Today we’re reading the very beginning — who showed up, where Jesus sat down, and the first words out of his mouth: eight blessings we call the Beatitudes.
Read Matthew 5:1–12 together. Go around the circle; each student reads one verse. Anyone who doesn’t want to read can say “pass” — no pressure.
Discuss:
Before Jesus even says a word, what kind of crowd is showing up? (look back at Matt 4:23–25) — Sick people. Hurting people. People from everywhere. Not the cool kids. Jesus uses the word “blessed” over and over. How is his idea of “blessed” different from the one on our board? Do you notice a pattern in how each Beatitude is worded? — Blessed are ___, for they will ___. A condition, then a promise. Look at verses 3 and 10. What do they have in common? — Same promise: theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Jesus bookends the whole list with it. The kingdom isn’t a someday thing — it’s here, already, for these people. Then walk through the eight blessings. Don’t lecture each one — ask, let them answer, fill in where needed.
“Poor in spirit” (v.3) — Spiritually empty-handed. Knowing you don’t have it all together. The opposite of proud. “Those who mourn” (v.4) — Why would Jesus bless grieving people? Because God is the God who comforts. “The meek” (v.5) — Meek isn’t weak. Meek is strength under control. Think a trained warhorse. Think Moses. Think Jesus. “Hunger and thirst for righteousness” (v.6) — A gut-level craving for what’s right. And the promise: they will be filled. “The merciful” (v.7) — Mercy isn’t just being nice. Mercy is giving someone what they don’t deserve — because God gave us what we didn’t deserve. “Pure in heart” (v.8) — Not “perfect.” Undivided. Same person online, at school, at home, at Crossroads. “Peacemakers” (v.9) — Not peace-keepers (who stay quiet to avoid a fight). Peace-makers step into conflict to heal it. That’s what God did for us. “Persecuted for righteousness” (vv.10–12) — Following Jesus sometimes costs something. Jesus says: rejoice — you’re in good company with the prophets. Say:
Here’s what Jesus is doing. He is flipping the world’s scoreboard upside down. The world cheers for people who have it all together. Jesus cheers for people who know they don’t. The world runs away from people who are grieving. Jesus runs toward them. The world worships the loudest person in the room. Jesus blesses the one who doesn’t need to be.
These eight blessings are going to change how we look at our school, our team, our lunch table — and our own hearts.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Thank God for the gift of the group, and ask the Holy Spirit to open hearts to the words of Jesus.
data.turn-toward-action--name-one#Turn Toward:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
On this card, write the name of one person in your life who fits Jesus’ list more than the world’s list. You don’t have to show anyone. Just write the name.
Slip it in your pocket, your phone case, or your Bible. Your job this week: pray for that person every day. And do one thing — literally one thing — that tells them they matter. Say hi. Save them a seat. Text them. Invite them. Anything.
End with prayer:
Jesus, thank you that your blessing doesn’t depend on the scoreboard of the world. That your blessing is given to the meek, the mourning, the peacemakers, and the pour in spirit; because that is who we are. Give us your eyes this week for the people on our cards. Amen.

data: Scripture: Matthew 5:13–16 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
Jesus has just finished the Beatitudes. Without so much as a breath, he pivots from “blessed are you…” to “you are the salt of the earth… you are the light of the world.” Notice what he didn’t say. He didn’t say try to be salt. He didn’t say if you get your act together, you might someday be light. He said you are. It’s an identity statement. The question isn’t whether your students are salt and light; the question is what kind of salt and light they’re being.
A few things worth knowing:
Verse 16 gives the reason: “let your light shine before others, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven.” Not so you get noticed. Not so you look holy. So God gets noticed. For middle schoolers, the quiet temptation is to keep faith private — something you do at church, not at the lunch table. Jesus is saying: that’s not an option. You are already salt. You are already light. The only question is whether the world gets to taste it and see it.
data.catch-their-attention--the-taste-test--t:
Before the lesson: Set out two identical bowls. Fill one with the unsalted version of the snack, fill the other with the salted version. Keep the difference secret.
During the lesson:
Part 1 — The taste test. Pass out the unsalted snack first. Say nothing. Let them chew. Wait for the reactions.
What are we tasting? Anything? Anyone want seconds?
Then pass out the salted version. Same food. Completely different experience.
What changed? One ingredient. That’s it.
Part 2 — The lamp. Turn off the overhead lights, or dim them. Hand a student a flashlight. Have them turn it on.
What’s this for?
Then take the flashlight and put it under the bowl.
How useful is it now?
Say:
Hold onto both of those. Tonight Jesus gives his followers two names — and they’re the two things we just messed with.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
You are salt and light. Your life is meant to make God visible and tasteable to the people around you — not so you get noticed, so God does.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Where in your life are you “hiding your light”? At school? At practice? Online? Around certain friends? What would “salty” friendship look like for you this week? Not preachy — actually flavored. Who in your life is genuinely salt and light? Someone whose life makes you want to know God more? Jesus says we shine so others will glorify God. What’s the difference between making yourself look good and making God look good?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student A taste test: two small bowls of the same snack (plain popcorn, unsalted tortilla chips, plain pretzels) — one unsalted, one salted. Keep them hidden at first. A flashlight + a cereal-bowl-sized container to cover it with Whiteboard or large paper + markers Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-51316#Read a:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Last week we sat in the Beatitudes — Jesus cheering for the poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek. Without even stopping for breath, he pivots and says two of the most famous lines in the whole sermon. Listen for the pattern.
Read Matthew 5:13–16 together. Go around the circle; each student reads 1–2 verses.
Discuss:
What does salt do? — Preserves. Flavors. Wakes up bland food. Wakes up bland anything. Salt fact: In the ancient world, salt was essential for life. It preserved food before refrigeration. It flavored what would otherwise be bland. It was so valuable that Roman soldiers were sometimes paid in salt (hence salary). Salt that’s lost its saltiness — contaminated, diluted — is useless. Jesus is saying: if a follower of mine loses the thing that makes them distinct, what’s the point? What does light do? — Reveals. Guides. Warms. You can’t hide from it. Light fact: In the ancient world, light wasn’t a flip of a switch. An oil lamp was essential gear. A city on a hillside at night was visible for miles. You don’t light a lamp just to hide it under a bowl — that’s absurd. Light has a purpose: to be seen. Jesus says salt that’s lost its saltiness is “no longer good for anything, except to be thrown out.” What does he mean? — If a follower of Jesus loses the thing that makes them distinct, there’s nothing there. “A town built on a hill cannot be hidden.” (v.14) What’s that like today? — You can’t miss a lit-up stadium at night. You can’t miss a stage in a dark auditorium. Light gets noticed. Look at verse 16. WHY do we let our light shine? — So people see our good deeds and give glory to our Father. Not to us. The whole point is to point people to God. Say:
Here’s the thing Jesus refuses to let us do: keep our faith private. He doesn’t say “be salty at church” or “light up for youth group.” He says “you are the salt of the earth” and “you are the light of the world.” Your whole week. Your whole school. Your whole table. Your whole team.
And the point — the whole point — isn’t to make you look like a good kid. It’s to make God look like the good God he actually is. When your life tastes different and shines different, people get curious about who’s behind it.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Ask God that this group wouldn’t be Sunday-only Christians — that we’d be the kind of salt and light that changes the flavor of our school this week.
data.turn-toward-action--one-place-this-week#:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
Two questions. Write the answers on your card.
1. ONE place this week I can be salt and light on purpose. (Your lunch table. Your team bus. That one group chat. One specific class.)
2. ONE specific thing I’ll do there. (Say hi to the kid who’s alone. Thank a teacher on your way out. Refuse to join the roast. Invite someone to Crossroads.)
Keep the card in your pocket, bible, or phone case. Look at it on your way to school.
Close with prayer. Pray that this group wouldn’t just come to church — that this group would be church, all week, in the places we already spend our time.

data: Scripture: Matthew 5:17–48 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.antithesis-1--anger-matt-52124#Antithesi:
Read it. Ask:
What’s the old rule? — Don’t murder. What does Jesus add? — Don’t even nurse anger. Don’t call people names. Reconcile. Jesus actually says: if you’re bringing a gift to God and you remember your brother has something against you, leave the gift and go make it right first. Why would Jesus put reconciliation BEFORE worship? — Because you can’t worship a God of love while you’re hating your brother. Real worship and real relationships are tied together.
data.antithesis-2--lust--guarding-the-eyes-ma:
Read it. Say up front — this one can feel awkward. That’s okay. Jesus is taking it seriously; so are we.
Ask:
What’s the old rule? — Don’t cheat on your spouse. What does Jesus push past the rule to? — What your eyes are doing. What your heart is doing before a single action happens. In 5:29–30 Jesus talks about cutting off the hand or plucking out the eye. Is he being literal? — No. He’s using shock language to make a point: take this seriously. Whatever you’re letting your eyes feed on is shaping your heart. For a middle schooler, what does this passage mean practically? — What you scroll past. What shows you stream. What you DM. What you save. Jesus is saying: don’t just avoid the “line” — guard what your eyes feed on.
data.antithesis-3--love-your-enemies-matt-543:
Read it. This is the wild one.
Ask:
What’s the old rule? — Love your neighbor. Hate your enemy. What does Jesus replace it with? — Love your enemies. Pray for those who persecute you. Why does Jesus say this makes us like our Father? — Because God sends rain on the evil and the good. He gives sun to people who don’t love him back. If we only love people who love us, we’re doing nothing different than the world. Who’s an “enemy” in your life? Not someone trying to kill you — someone who’s been unkind, who left you out, who made fun of you, who makes your life harder. Close the section with v.48:
“Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect.”
Explain:
The word “perfect” here doesn’t mean flawless. It’s a Greek word that means mature, whole, complete. Jesus is saying: grow up into the whole person your Father made you to be. Love like he loves. Be honest like he’s honest. Stay patient like he stays patient. Not flawless. Whole.
Say:
Every one of these teachings pushes past behavior to the heart. The world says, “Just don’t cross the line.” Jesus says, “Don’t even want to. Let me give you a new heart instead.”
That’s not harder religion. That’s a different kind of religion. It’s not about white-knuckling the rules. It’s about a heart that’s been made new from the inside.
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
After the Beatitudes (Lesson 1) and salt-and-light (Lesson 2), Jesus goes in. He makes a huge statement: “Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have come not to abolish them but to fulfill them.” (v.17) Then, six times in a row, he pulls the same move: “You have heard that it was said… but I say to you…”
The six antitheses are:
Murder → anger (vv.21–26) — Don’t just not murder. Don’t even nurse a grudge. Go reconcile. Adultery → lust (vv.27–30) — Don’t just not cheat. Don’t even look at a person as a thing. Divorce (vv.31–32) — Don’t treat marriage as disposable. (Worth knowing, but for middle school this can be mentioned briefly without camping out.) Oaths (vv.33–37) — Don’t need fancy vows. Be the kind of person whose yes means yes. Eye for eye → turn the other cheek (vv.38–42) — Don’t play the revenge game. Break the cycle. Love neighbor, hate enemy → love your enemies (vv.43–48) — The wildest one. Love the people who actively hurt you. The climax is v.48: “Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect.” The Greek word for “perfect” there is teleios — it means mature, complete, whole. Not flawless. Jesus isn’t telling middle schoolers to be sinless. He’s telling them to grow into the whole-people God made them to be.
This lesson’s big move: Jesus isn’t raising the bar; he’s going underneath it. The world’s idea of being good is about behavior — don’t cross the line. Jesus cares about the heart — don’t even want to cross it. That’s not harder religion. That’s a new heart.
Three antitheses will give middle schoolers plenty: anger, guarding the eyes, loving enemies. Save divorce, oaths, and retaliation for teacher notes — you can reference them briefly, but don’t stretch the lesson thin.
data.catch-their-attention--surface-vs-heart-:
Before the lesson: On the whiteboard, draw a big iceberg. Draw a wavy waterline across the middle. Label the top third SURFACE and the bottom two-thirds HEART.
During the lesson: Say:
Every day of your life, two of you are going at the same time. There’s surface-you — what people see, what you post, how you act — and there’s heart-you — what’s actually going on underneath.
Ask the group:
Give me some things that live on the “surface.” (How I dress. The GPA. The followers. The team I’m on. How I act at youth group.) Now things that live “below the waterline.” (Anger. Jealousy. What I look at when no one’s watching. Grudges. Anxiety. What I actually believe about myself.) Write their answers in the right zones.
Say:
Most rules in the world — at school, at home, online — only care about the top half. Don’t get caught. Don’t cross the line. Look fine. Jesus walks into the Sermon on the Mount and says: I care about the whole iceberg.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
Jesus doesn’t just want you to follow the rules. He wants your heart.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
What’s harder for you — not doing bad stuff, or not wanting to do it? Is there someone in your life right now who’s gotten under your skin? What would “going to reconcile” look like? What are your eyes feeding on that you know is messing with your heart? Jesus said love your enemies. Who pops into your mind? What would it look like to pray for them by name this week? What’s one “line” in your life that you don’t cross… but maybe God cares more about what your heart is doing before it gets there?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student Whiteboard or large paper + markers Blue painter’s tape (optional — to mark a “waterline” on the wall for the iceberg illustration) Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-51748#Read a:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Jesus is about to do something wild. He’s going to take rules that everyone in the room already knew — rules from the Old Testament — and he’s going to push each one deeper. Watch for the phrase “You have heard it said… but I say to you.” He does this six times. We’re going to camp on three of them.
Read Matthew 5:17–20 to set the stage. Jesus is not throwing out the Law. He’s fulfilling it.
Discuss briefly:
Some people think Jesus came to scrap the Old Testament. What does he actually say? — “I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them.” What do you think it means for Jesus to fulfill the Law? — He completes it. He shows us what it was always pointing to. He makes it more than rule-following — he makes it love. Then take three antitheses.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Tonight’s teaching goes to the heart — pray that the Holy Spirit would make us brave enough to go there with Jesus.
data.turn-toward-action--one-heart-move#Turn:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
Jesus wants more than your behavior. He wants your heart. Tonight, pick one heart-level move. ONE.
Write on the card:
One person I’ll reconcile with this week (even if it’s just a text). One thing I’ll stop feeding my eyes (an app limit, an unfollow, a scroll habit). One enemy I’ll pray for by name every day this week. Pick one of those three. Write which one. Write the first concrete step.
Close with prayer. Pray that God would give this group new hearts — not just better behavior. Pray Ezekiel 36:26 out loud if you want a scripture to close with: “I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you.”

data: Scripture: Matthew 6:5–15 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
Matthew 6 opens with Jesus warning about “acts of righteousness” performed for show (v.1). He works through three of them — giving (vv.2–4), praying (vv.5–15), and fasting (vv.16–18) — and each time he says the same thing: don’t do this for the camera. Do it in the quiet, for an audience of One.
Tonight we camp on prayer. A few things worth holding onto:
Verses 5–6 — Don’t pray to be seen. Jesus isn’t against public prayer (he led it himself). He’s against prayer as a performance. Go into your room, close the door, pray to your Father who sees in secret. Verses 7–8 — Don’t babble. Jesus names a specific failure: piling on words to manipulate God. He says: your Father already knows what you need before you ask. That’s actually a comfort, not a criticism. Prayer isn’t informing God. It’s connecting with him. Verses 9–13 — The Lord’s Prayer. This is the model. Six short petitions. Nothing fancy. It takes 20 seconds to pray. Verses 14–15 — Jesus returns to one line of the prayer and underlines it: forgive as you have been forgiven. The forgiveness we offer mirrors the forgiveness we’ve received. It’s not that forgiveness is earned — it’s that a forgiven heart can’t stay unforgiving. The Lord’s Prayer, phrase by phrase:
“Our Father in heaven” — Prayer begins with relationship, not religion. “Father” (Abba, in Mark’s parallel) is how a child addresses a dad. “Our” — not just mine, but ours; the church prays together. “Hallowed be your name” — May your name be treated as holy. A plea that God’s reputation wouldn’t be cheapened — starting with my own life. “Your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as it is in heaven” — Not my will first. Yours. Heaven’s rhythms breaking into earth. “Give us today our daily bread” — Real, practical, today-sized needs. Not a retirement plan — a lunch. “Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors” — The hinge. We receive mercy in the same measure we extend it (which is why v.14–15 underlines this). “Lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one” — Protection. Keep us out of places where we know we’ll crumble. The big idea: Jesus is de-scaring prayer. Middle schoolers often think prayer is performance, or that they’ll say the wrong thing, or that they need fancy words. Jesus says: stop. You already have a Father who already knows. Talk to him like a kid.
A pastoral word on the word “Father.” For some students, “Father” is a loaded word. Their earthly father may be absent, angry, unsafe, or simply unknown. When Jesus teaches us to pray “Our Father in heaven,” he is not saying “God is exactly like your dad.” He’s saying “God is the Father you were always meant to have.” If you have a student in the room for whom “Father” lands heavy, don’t rush past it — name it out loud. Jesus is offering a Father who is perfectly caring, patient, forgiving, and present. That’s the Father every kid in the room deserves, and it’s the one they have in God.
data.catch-their-attention--the-big-show-pray:
Before the lesson: Ask another leader, or a student who can play along, to help. You’ll do two prayers back-to-back.
During the lesson:
Stand up, grab the microphone prop (or pretend). Say:
Okay, I’m going to pray. Get ready. This one’s gonna be powerful.
Then pray out loud, ridiculously over the top:
“O most high, magnificent, awesome, incredible God of the universe, who sits enthroned above the heavens, whose majesty is beyond words and whose glory knows no bounds, I come before you humbly, so humbly, so super humbly, to thank you and to praise you and to lift up this assembled gathering on this Wednesday evening…”
Keep going as long as the students will tolerate it.
Then without commentary, turn to the other leader/student. Say: “Your turn.”
They pray:
“God, thanks for this group. Help us hear you tonight. Amen.”
Look at the students:
Which one do you think Jesus likes better?
Let them answer. Then say:
Before we read tonight, I want to tell you this. Jesus has something very specific to say about how to pray — and it’s not what most people think.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
Prayer is a kid talking to a Father who already loves them. Short. Honest. Trusting. No performance required.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
What makes prayer feel awkward or hard for you — if it does? Jesus says “WHEN you pray,” not “if you pray.” Why do you think he assumes his followers are praying? Which phrase of the Lord’s Prayer hits different now that we’ve slowed it down? Why? Be honest: is there someone you’re asking God to forgive you, but you haven’t forgiven? What do you think Jesus would say to you about that? What’s one thing you don’t pray about right now — that you probably could?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student Whiteboard or large paper + markers Optional: a microphone or large prop (used for the “performance prayer” demo) Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening) Optional: pre-printed cards of the Lord’s Prayer for students to take home
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-6515#Read an:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Jesus is in the middle of the Sermon on the Mount. He’s been talking about not doing good stuff for the camera. Now he zeroes in on one specific thing: prayer. He tells us two things NOT to do, and then — because Jesus is a good teacher — he shows us what to do instead.
Read Matthew 6:5–15 together. Go around the circle.
Discuss:
In verses 5–6, what does Jesus say NOT to do when you pray? — Don’t pray to be seen. Don’t pray to impress. Go into your room, close the door. In verses 7–8, what’s the second thing Jesus says not to do? — Don’t babble. Don’t pile on words. Your Father already knows what you need. Does “your Father already knows what you need” make prayer more or less important? Why? — More. It means prayer isn’t us informing God. It’s us connecting with God. He already knows — he wants to hear from you anyway. Now read verses 9–13 again slowly. Notice anything? — It’s short. Almost weirdly short. Jesus is modeling what he just said. Then walk the Lord’s Prayer phrase by phrase on the whiteboard. Write each phrase and stop to unpack it.
“Our Father in heaven” — Who are we talking to? Father. Not boss. Not cop. Not distant king. Dad. And “Our” — the whole family praying together. Pause here if needed. Acknowledge the room: some of us have great dads, some of us don’t. Jesus isn’t saying God is like your dad. He’s saying God is the Father every one of you was made for — perfectly patient, perfectly present, perfectly kind. If “Father” is hard for you, stay with it. This is the one who doesn’t disappoint. “Hallowed be your name” — “May your name be treated as holy.” Starting with my own life. Not dragging God’s name through the mud by how I live. “Your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as it is in heaven” — Whose will comes first? Not mine. His. This is the line that makes prayer dangerous — because when you pray it and mean it, it changes what you want. “Give us today our daily bread” — Real needs. Today’s needs. Not a five-year plan — a lunch, a ride, the algebra test. “Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors” — The big one. We ask God to forgive us in the same way we forgive others. Jesus comes back to this in verses 14–15 to underline it. “Lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one” — Protection. Keep me out of the places where I know I fall apart. Read verses 14–15 together again.
Ask:
Why does Jesus circle back and underline this one specific line? — Because a forgiven heart can’t stay unforgiving. If we’re walking around receiving God’s mercy but refusing to give any, something’s not wired right. Say:
Here’s what Jesus just handed us. Prayer isn’t performance. You don’t score points with God by using big words. You don’t impress him with volume. You don’t manipulate him with repetition. You walk in as a kid, say what you mean, and trust your Father to hear you — because he already knows and he already loves you.
And when he teaches us the model, he keeps it short. Six petitions. Twenty seconds. But every phrase is doing work.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Pray a very short, very simple prayer out loud yourself — something like: “Jesus, thanks for this group. Help us to hear you tonight. Amen.” This models the whole lesson.
data.turn-toward-action--pray-it-for-a-week#T:
Hand each student an index card (or a pre-printed Lord’s Prayer card if you have them). Say:
Here’s your challenge. Pray the Lord’s Prayer every day this week. In bed. At breakfast. On the bus. In class before a test. Doesn’t matter where. Just pray it.
Don’t rush it. Don’t mumble through it. Actually mean each phrase. See what happens after a week.
Pray the Lord’s Prayer together as a whole group to close the lesson:
Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name, your kingdom come, your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. Give us today our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one. Amen.

data: Scripture: Matthew 6:19–24 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
Jesus has just finished talking about prayer (last week). Before he gets to do not worry (next week), he drops six of the most quoted verses in the whole sermon — and ties them together with a single idea: what has your heart has your life.
Verses 19–21 — Two Treasures. “Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moths and vermin destroy, and where thieves break in and steal. But store up for yourselves treasures in heaven… For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.” “Treasures on earth” — anything that can be eaten by moths, rusted by rain, or stolen by a thief. It decays. It runs out. It can be taken. “Treasures in heaven” — things that last. Things God cares about. Love, faithfulness, mercy, the people you’ve helped, the forgiveness you’ve extended. Verse 21 is the punchline. Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also. You follow your treasure like a magnet. Whatever you’re collecting, you’re becoming. Verses 22–23 — The Lamp of the Eye. “The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eyes are healthy, your whole body will be full of light. But if your eyes are unhealthy, your whole body will be full of darkness.” Ancient metaphor: your eyes are the windows your body takes in light through. What you look at determines what fills you up. Verse 24 — Two Masters. “No one can serve two masters… You cannot serve both God and money.” The word Jesus uses for money is mammon — it’s personified almost like a god. Jesus isn’t saying money is bad. He’s saying money makes a terrible god. You can use money. You can’t serve it. The thread: Jesus is asking a penetrating question — what are you living for? What you store, what you look at, and who you serve all expose the same thing: your heart. And middle schoolers are already being shaped by what they’re collecting, watching, and worshipping. They just don’t always call it that.
data.catch-their-attention--the-burning-house:
Say:
Quick scenario. Your house is on fire. Everyone in your family is already out safe. The pets are out. You have exactly sixty seconds to run back in and grab ONE thing before the roof caves in. What do you grab?
Let every student answer. Write the answers on the whiteboard.
After the list is up, ask:
Look at this list. What does it say about us that these are the things we’d run back in for?
Say:
Whatever you would run back in for — that’s a treasure. Tonight Jesus has something to say about what kind of treasure will actually last.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
Whatever has your heart has your life. You cannot serve both God and money — pick one.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Based on how you spend your time, your money, and your attention — what are you actually treasuring right now? What’s one thing you look at a lot that you know is filling you with darkness, not light? Jesus says you can’t serve God AND money. Is there another “master” you’ve been low-key serving? (Popularity? Grades? A sport? A person?) If your heart really follows your treasure — what do you want your heart to become over the next year? Verse 21 says it bluntly: your heart goes where your treasure is. What does that mean you need to change about what you’re collecting?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student A physical “treasure pile” prop: a mix of stuff that decays/can-be-stolen (fake cash, a trophy, a phone, a jersey, a rusted old coin) and stuff that doesn’t (a Bible, a card with a person’s name, a cross, a photo of people serving) Whiteboard or large paper + markers Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-61924#Read a:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Jesus is in the middle of the Sermon on the Mount. He just taught us how to pray. Now, before he gets to that famous passage about not worrying, he drops six verses that hit HARD. Listen for a single idea that runs through all three parts.
Read Matthew 6:19–24 together. Go around the circle.
Discuss:
Treasures (vv.19–21):
What are “treasures on earth”? Why does Jesus say not to store them up? — Stuff that rots, rusts, or gets stolen. It runs out. It can be taken. What are “treasures in heaven”? What lasts forever? — The people you helped. The mercy you showed. The forgiveness you gave. Your relationship with God. The ways you made God look good in the world. Verse 21 — “Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.” What does that mean? — You follow your treasure. Whatever you’re collecting, you start to love. Whatever you love, you start to become. The Lamp of the Eye (vv.22–23): 4. Jesus says the eye is the lamp of the body. What do you think he means? — What you look at fills you up. If you feed your eyes garbage, your insides go dark. If you feed your eyes on good stuff, you fill with light. 5. For a middle schooler, what are your eyes on most? (Be honest.) What’s filling you up right now?
Two Masters (v.24): 6. Jesus says you can’t serve TWO masters. Why not? — Because both want your whole heart. You end up loving one and hating the other. 7. Jesus specifically names ONE master that competes with God. What is it? — Money. And not just cash — “mammon,” the whole system of chasing stuff to feel secure or important. 8. Is Jesus saying money is evil? — No. He’s saying money makes a terrible god. You can use money. You can’t serve it.
Say:
Jesus is asking us one question three different ways tonight. What are you living for?
What you store up shows your heart. What you look at fills your heart. Who you serve owns your heart. And you can’t split your heart — the world says you can, Jesus says you can’t.
Here’s what nobody’s going to tell you at school: the stuff the world tells you to collect — the grades, the followers, the shoes, the spot on the team, the version of your body that’s in this year — all of it will rust, break, fade, or get forgotten. Every bit of it. Jesus isn’t saying those things are evil. He’s saying they make terrible treasures. They can’t hold your heart.
But the things God asks us to store up — love for the kid nobody sits with, forgiveness for the one who hurt us, time with him, faithfulness when no one sees — that stuff lasts forever.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Ask God to show each student where their treasure actually is tonight.
data.turn-toward-action--move-some-treasure#T:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
Three quick moves this week. Pick the one God is putting in front of you.
Move something up to heaven. Spend time, money, or energy this week on someone who can’t pay you back. A sibling. A classmate. A kid nobody sits with. Fast your eyes from something. One app limit, one unfollow, one show you turn off. Something you know is filling you with darkness. Fire one of your masters. Name a thing that’s been running your life in secret — grades, a person’s opinion, followers. Out loud, tell God you’re not serving it anymore. Ask him to be your one master instead. Write which one you picked and the first step.
Close with prayer. Read Matt 6:21 out loud one more time on your way out:
“For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.”

data: Scripture: Matthew 6:25–34 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
Middle school is a worry factory. Grades. Friend drama. Body stuff. Family stuff. Tests. The lunch table. That group chat. The future. What people think. What God thinks. The pile never stops growing.
Jesus knows this. In Matthew 6:25–34, he walks right into the middle of it and says one of the most direct, pastoral things in the whole Sermon on the Mount: “Therefore I tell you, do not worry.”
A few things to know before you teach:
The “therefore” matters. This passage flows directly out of Matt 6:19–24, where Jesus says you cannot serve both God and money. So when he says “therefore do not worry,” he’s not handing out an inspirational quote. He’s saying: if your treasure is in heaven and your master is God, worry doesn’t fit anymore. Jesus gives two illustrations — both from nature. The birds (v.26): They don’t plant, harvest, or store anything. God feeds them anyway. “Are you not much more valuable than they?” The lilies (vv.28–30): They don’t work or sew clothes, and yet Solomon at his richest was never dressed like one. “If that is how God clothes the grass of the field, will he not much more clothe you?” Worry is useless. Verse 27: “Can any one of you by worrying add a single hour to your life?” Jesus isn’t dismissing what students feel — he’s pointing out that worry doesn’t actually do anything. The pivot is verse 33. Don’t just not worry — instead, seek first his kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be given to you as well. Worry is replaced by a different orientation entirely. Trust, and a different priority. Verse 34 is the middle-school mic drop. “Therefore do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself. Each day has enough trouble of its own.” Middle schoolers don’t need to be scolded for worrying. They need to hear that their Father knows what they need, has been feeding sparrows and clothing grass for a very long time, and is still paying attention to them.
data.catch-their-attention--the-worry-jar#Cat:
Before the lesson: Set the jar or box on the table with a stack of slips and pens nearby. Place the flower/plant and a printed bird picture beside it — but don’t call attention to them yet.
During the lesson: Hand every student a slip of paper. Say:
You’ve got sixty seconds. No names. No sharing. Write down the top two or three things weighing on you right now. The stuff that pops into your head when you can’t fall asleep. The worries that hang out in the back of your mind during class.
Give them a full minute. Then ask them to fold the slips and drop them in the jar without looking at anyone else’s. Don’t read them aloud.
Say:
Every one of those worries is riding on your shoulders right now. Tonight Jesus has something to say about that pile — and it’s not what you might think.
Gesture at the flower and the bird picture.
Keep an eye on these two. They’re gonna come back.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
You have a Father who already knows what you need. Worry isn’t the move. Trust is.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
What’s the biggest thing you worry about right now? (You don’t have to say out loud — but be honest with yourself.) Has worrying about that thing ever actually fixed it? Or does it just keep it on repeat? What would “seeking first the kingdom” look like for a middle schooler this week? Not abstractly — concretely. Be honest: do you actually believe God knows what you need? Or does it feel like he forgot about you? Jesus ends with “tomorrow will worry about itself.” What’s one worry about tomorrow you could set down tonight?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student A jar, shoebox, or empty tissue box (the “worry jar”) Small slips of paper or sticky notes (one per student, plus extras) A small flower, plant, or even a photo of a field in bloom A photo or video of birds (YouTube clip works, but keep it short — under a minute) Whiteboard or large paper + markers Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-62534#Read a:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Quick recap: Jesus has been preaching his famous Sermon on the Mount. Right before tonight’s passage, he told everyone you can’t serve both God and money — you have to pick. Then he drops this word: “therefore.” That word matters. It means what he’s about to say is built on what he just said.
Read Matthew 6:25–34 together, going around the circle.
Discuss:
What’s the very first command Jesus gives here in v.25? — Do not worry. What two illustrations does he use? Why those? — Birds and flowers (lilies). Both are cared for by God without trying. Jesus is saying: if God takes care of sparrows and grass, he’s obviously paying attention to you. Verse 27 — “Can any one of you by worrying add a single hour to your life?” What’s Jesus getting at? — Worry doesn’t actually fix anything. It feels productive. It isn’t. In v.32 Jesus says the “pagans” run after food, drink, and clothes. What’s he saying about what makes Christians different? — Not what we have — what we trust. The pagan strategy is to chase these things and panic. The Christian strategy is to trust the Father who already knows what we need. Verse 33 — what does Jesus say to do instead of worrying? — Seek first his kingdom and his righteousness. What does that actually mean for a middle schooler? — Make God’s priorities your first priorities. Live the way he’s teaching you to live. Trust him to handle the stuff you can’t. Verse 34 — “Don’t worry about tomorrow.” Why do you think Jesus adds that? — Because most worry is about tomorrow. It hasn’t even happened yet. Today has enough going on. Stay here. Say:
Worry feels like we’re caring — like if we just worry hard enough, something gets fixed. Jesus says: nope. Worry is a treadmill. It burns a ton of energy and gets you nowhere.
Here’s his logic: your Father in heaven feeds every bird in the sky and colors every wildflower in the field. He has been doing that for longer than anyone reading this has been alive. Are you really gonna tell me he forgot about you?
And then Jesus pivots. He doesn’t just say stop worrying. He gives us something to do instead: seek first his kingdom. Trust him with the stuff you can’t control. Put his priorities first. Live today like he’s actually your Father. And tomorrow? Tomorrow will worry about itself.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Tonight is about a pile of real worries — ask the Holy Spirit to come close to every anxious middle schooler in the room.
data.turn-toward-action--hand-it-over#Turn To:
Pick up the worry jar. Don’t read the slips. Just hold it up.
Say:
These are yours. Every one of these worries is real. Jesus isn’t saying they’re silly. He’s saying — hand them over to a Father who’s been feeding the sparrows for a long time.
Hand each student a fresh index card. Have them write:
ONE worry I’m handing to God this week. (Be specific.) ONE kingdom-first thing I’ll do instead of spinning on that worry. (Pray about it and let it go when the thought comes back. Text a friend instead of doom-scrolling. Read a Psalm before bed. Show up to Crossroads when I’d rather stay home.) Close together. Pray over the jar. Thank God that he already knows. Ask him to take the pile. Close with Philippians 4:6–7 if you want to read one more scripture: “Do not be anxious about anything… and the peace of God, which transcends all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus.”

data: Scripture: Matthew 7:1–6 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
Matthew 7:1 is the most often-quoted verse by people who don’t actually go to church. “Do not judge, or you too will be judged.” It gets used to mean “don’t ever say anything is right or wrong” — which is almost the opposite of what Jesus is saying.
Here’s what Jesus actually packs into these six verses:
Verse 1–2: “Do not judge, or you too will be judged. For in the same way you judge others, you will be judged, and with the measure you use, it will be measured to you.” Jesus isn’t banning moral discernment — he literally just told us (earlier in the sermon) to watch for false prophets and guard our hearts. What he’s banning is the posture of the judge. The smug, stand-above, contempt kind of judging. He’s warning: whatever yardstick you use on people will come back around on you. Verses 3–5 — The Speck and the Plank. This is the comedic-on-purpose centerpiece. Jesus paints a ridiculous picture: you have a two-by-four sticking out of your face and you’re leaning in to fix a speck of sawdust in someone else’s eye. It’s absurd. It’s meant to be. Jesus is not saying “never help your brother or sister” — he says “first take the plank out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother’s eye.” Self-examination before correction, not instead of it. Verse 6 — “Do not give dogs what is sacred; do not throw your pearls to pigs.” This feels like a sudden pivot, but Jesus is balancing his point. He’s not saying “accept everything, call nothing wrong.” He’s saying: use discernment about what’s precious. Some people aren’t ready to hear truth, and forcing it can do harm. Judgment is out; discernment is in. The core idea for middle schoolers: you can care enough about someone to help them see clearly — but first you have to deal with what you’re not seeing about yourself. The move isn’t don’t judge anything. The move is don’t judge from a throne you don’t belong on.
Middle school is a ruthless ranking machine. Judgment is the native language. Jesus is inviting a different posture — humble, self-aware, merciful.
data.catch-their-attention--plank-in-your-eye:
Before the lesson: Have the 2×4 (or yardstick) lying in plain sight. Place a tiny crumb or grain of something on a napkin nearby.
During the lesson: Hold up the tiny speck dramatically.
Okay. Everyone look really hard at this. What is it?
Let them try to identify it. Then pick up the 2×4.
Now. Imagine I have this sticking out of my face. Like, right in my eye. Right now. Big 2×4, just… bam, in the eye. And instead of dealing with that, I’m over here trying to pick that tiny speck out of your eyelashes. How do you think that’s gonna go?
Let them laugh. Make it goofy.
Hold onto that picture. Jesus actually tells this joke tonight. And he means it.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
The way you judge is the way you’ll be judged. Check the plank in your own eye before you go after the speck in anyone else’s.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Have you ever had somebody call you out for something when they were clearly doing the same thing ten times worse? How did that feel? What’s a “plank” you’ve been carrying that’s way bigger than the “specks” you’re always noticing in other people? Middle school is built on judgment — roasts, rankings, the unspoken lunch-table pecking order. What would it look like if you refused to play? Is there someone you’ve been quietly judging this week whose story you don’t actually know? Jesus says “the measure you use will be measured to you.” If someone used YOUR yardstick on YOU this week, how would it go?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student A 2×4 piece of wood (or even a yardstick — something obviously big) and a tiny speck of something (a single grain of sawdust, a crumb, a pencil shaving) Whiteboard or large paper + markers Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-716#Read and:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Matthew 7:1 is the most misused verse of the entire Bible. You’ve probably heard someone quote it to mean “don’t ever say anything is right or wrong.” That’s not what Jesus is saying. Listen carefully — Jesus is going after a specific kind of judging, and he’s hilarious about it.
Read Matthew 7:1–6 together.
Discuss:
Verses 1–2:
What does Jesus warn is going to happen to us if we judge others harshly? — The same measure gets used on us. The yardstick you hold up on others will come back and land on you. What does that tell us about what kind of judging Jesus is against? — The smug, above-it-all kind. Not the kind that says “that’s not right.” The kind that says “…and I would never.” Verses 3–5: 3. Draw the picture Jesus is painting. — A person with a 2×4 sticking out of their face, leaning in to help someone with a tiny piece of sawdust in their eye. 4. Why do you think Jesus makes this sound so ridiculous? — Because it IS. We do it all the time, and it’s absurd. We’ll rip on someone for a flaw we’re twice as guilty of. 5. Jesus doesn’t say “never help your brother.” What does he say? — First take the plank out of your own eye. Then you’ll see clearly to help.
Verse 6: 6. This one sounds weird after the speck-and-plank stuff. What does it balance? — Jesus isn’t saying “never discern anything.” He’s saying: use wisdom about what’s precious. Don’t force truth on people who aren’t ready. Judgment is out; discernment is in.
Say:
Here’s what Jesus is doing. He is not saying, “never call anything wrong.” He’s saying, “drop the gavel.” The seat of judgment is not for you. It’s not for me. It’s for God.
That doesn’t mean we don’t care about what’s right. It doesn’t mean we let our friends crash. It just means — start with yourself. When you feel the urge to go after someone else, first ask: what’s the plank I’m carrying? What am I doing that’s way bigger than what I’m upset about?
Because here’s the wild thing Jesus promises. Once you pull your plank? You’ll actually see clearly enough to help. Judgment makes you blind. Humility makes you a good friend.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Tonight’s lesson cuts — ask the Holy Spirit to make us less interested in other people’s specks and more honest about our own planks.
data.turn-toward-action--plank-check#Turn Tow:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
Two sides of the card. One side is honest. One side is the move.
Side 1:
One speck I’ve been harping on in someone else’s life. One plank I’ve been ignoring in my own. Side 2: 3. One thing I’m going to do this week to pull my plank — apologize, confess, get help, talk to someone, pray about it honestly.
If you’ve been harping on someone else, the card is also a little reminder: pull your plank first.
Close with prayer. Ask God for eyes that see our own stuff clearly and eyes that are slower to judge everyone else.

data: Scripture: Matthew 7:7–12 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
Six verses. Three huge ideas. Jesus is bringing the Sermon on the Mount in for a landing, and the thread holding these verses together is one word: Father.
Verses 7–8 — Ask. Seek. Knock. “Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you.” All three verbs in the Greek are present tense and imperative — keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking. Jesus isn’t describing a one-time prayer formula. He’s describing a posture of ongoing dependence. Verses 9–11 — The Father Logic. “Which of you, if your son asks for bread, will give him a stone? Or if he asks for a fish, will give him a snake? If you, then, though you are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good gifts to those who ask him!” This is the heart of the passage. Jesus argues from the lesser to the greater. Even broken, imperfect human parents instinctively give their kids bread, not stones. How much more will your perfect heavenly Father give what you need? He’s not saying we’ll always get what we want — he’s saying we’ll get good gifts, because our Father knows what’s good. Verse 12 — The Golden Rule. “So in everything, do to others what you would have them do to you, for this sums up the Law and the Prophets.” The “so” is crucial. Because we have a Father who gives good gifts — because we’re kids who have received generosity we didn’t earn — we can afford to treat others with the same generosity. The Golden Rule isn’t a standalone moral principle. It’s the overflow of being a child of a generous Father. Two pastoral things to hold:
Jesus is not teaching a vending-machine gospel. “Ask and it will be given” doesn’t mean every prayer gets a yes. It means God hears, and God answers with good gifts — gifts shaped by his wisdom, not our wish list. The “Father” language here is the same loaded word you flagged in Lesson 4. Don’t skip past it. This entire passage rests on the premise that God is a good Father — and some of your students need to hear, again, that this is the Father they actually have.
data.catch-their-attention--knock-knock#Catch:
Before the lesson: Pick a door the group can see. If you have a co-leader, have them wait on the other side.
During the lesson: Point at the door. Say:
I want to try something. [Pick a student.] Stand up. Walk over to that door. Now — knock on it like you really want to get in.
Let them knock. The leader on the other side opens the door and says warmly: “Come on in.”
That was easy. Now imagine you spent years never knocking on that door. Never asking to be let in. Never even walking up. How weird would that be — if the person on the other side was ready to open it the whole time?
Say:
Jesus is about to tell us something about a door. Your Father is on the other side of it. He’s been waiting. You can knock.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
You have a good Father who loves to be asked. And because you’ve received so much, you can afford to treat other people the way you want to be treated.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
What’s something you’ve stopped asking God for because you don’t really think he’d answer? Why? When you pray, do you treat God more like a vending machine, a distant boss, or a good Father? Why? Who in your life treats you better than you deserve? What does it feel like? The Golden Rule in a middle-school hallway — what would change if you actually did that this week? Jesus says the Golden Rule sums up the whole Old Testament. If it sums up yours — how are you doing?
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student A loaf of bread + a rock + a gummy fish + a rubber snake (for the Father-logic visual). Bread + stone is the minimum; fish + snake doubles the impact. A closed door to knock on (literally) for the hook Whiteboard or large paper + markers Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-7712#Read an:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
Six verses. Jesus is wrapping up the Sermon on the Mount, and he packs a TON into these six. Listen for one word he keeps coming back to: Father. Everything in this passage hinges on it.
Read Matthew 7:7–12 together.
Discuss:
Verses 7–8 — Ask, Seek, Knock:
What three things does Jesus tell us to do? — Ask. Seek. Knock. Fun fact: in the original Greek, all three of these are verbs that mean keep doing it. Keep asking. Keep seeking. Keep knocking. Why do you think Jesus frames prayer as ongoing? — Because prayer isn’t a one-time form you submit. It’s a relationship. You don’t text your best friend once and call it a friendship. Verses 9–11 — The Father Logic: 3. Jesus uses a ridiculous example. Read verse 9 again. What does he ask? — If your kid asks for bread, are you gonna hand them a rock?
Hold up the bread and the rock. Make it stupid and obvious.
Why does Jesus use such an absurd example? — Because no decent parent in the world would do that. The question is supposed to be embarrassingly easy. Then Jesus says “IF YOU, THOUGH YOU ARE EVIL, know how to give good gifts…” What does that mean? — Even broken humans instinctively give their kids good stuff. How much more will a perfect Father do? What’s the “how much more” Jesus is arguing for? — If broken parents still give good gifts, God — the perfect Father — will absolutely give good gifts to those who ask. Pastoral note — the “Father” word again. Like in Lesson 4, don’t rush past this. If some students hear “Father” and flinch, name it out loud:
Some of us have dads who are amazing. Some of us have dads who aren’t. Jesus is not saying God is like your dad. He’s saying God is the Father every one of you was made to have. The kind who gives bread, not stones. Every time.
Does “ask and it will be given” mean I always get what I want? — No. It means God gives good gifts. Sometimes what you asked for wasn’t actually good. God’s answers are shaped by his wisdom, not your wish list. But he always hears. Verse 12 — The Golden Rule: 8. Now Jesus says “SO” — which means because of what he just said. How does the Golden Rule flow out of the Father stuff? — Because you’ve received generosity from your Father, you can afford to be generous with other people. Because you’ve been forgiven, you can forgive. You treat others the way you want to be treated because you’ve been treated that way first. 9. Jesus says “this sums up the Law and the Prophets.” That’s a huge claim. What does he mean? — He’s saying: the whole Old Testament boils down to this. Love God. Treat people the way you want to be treated. That’s it.
Say:
Jesus is handing us one of the most famous lines ever said — the Golden Rule. Do to others what you would have them do to you. You’ve heard it on posters, bumper stickers, maybe Ms. Whoever’s kindergarten wall. But notice where Jesus puts it. Right after he tells us our Father gives good gifts.
Because that’s where the Golden Rule actually works. When you’ve been given more than you deserve, you stop trying to ration what you give. When you know you have a generous Father, you can afford to be generous. When you’ve been loved first, you can love back.
Ask. Seek. Knock. And then overflow into the people around you.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Tonight’s passage is about a Father who loves being asked — so actually ask him to speak to this group tonight.
data.turn-toward-action--ask-and-overflow#Tur:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
Two sides tonight.
Side 1 — What I’m asking for: Write down one real thing you’re going to start asking, seeking, and knocking about this week. Something specific. Then commit: every morning or every night, bring this to your Father.
Side 2 — How I’m overflowing: Write down one specific person this week — and one thing you’re going to do for them that you’d want someone to do for you. Sit with them. Stand up for them. Text them. Forgive them. Include them.
Close with prayer. Before the closing prayer, let a few students say — if they want to — what they’re asking God for. Pray over those out loud.
End with:
Father, you love being asked. Your gifts are always good. Teach this group to keep knocking. And because we’ve received so much from you, help us overflow into every hallway this week. Amen.

data: Scripture: Matthew 7:24–29 Series: Sermon on the Mount
data.behind-the-curtain#Behind the Curtain:
What do I need to know before teaching?
This is the capstone. For eight weeks we’ve been climbing the mountain with Jesus — the Beatitudes, salt and light, the heart of the Law, prayer, treasure, trust, judgment, and asking. Tonight Jesus steps up to close his own sermon, and the way he wraps it is devastatingly simple: a parable about two builders.
Matthew 7:24–27 — both builders hear the same words. One puts them into practice and builds on the rock. The other hears but doesn’t act and builds on the sand. The storm comes for both. One house stands. The other falls with a crash.
A few things to hold onto before you teach:
The line between the wise and foolish builder is NOT knowledge. Both builders heard Jesus’ words. The line is practice. Hearing plus doing = rock. Hearing minus doing = sand. The storm is a given. Jesus doesn’t say if the rain falls — he says when. Every life gets storms. Middle school has plenty already; adulthood brings more. The question is never whether the rain comes. It’s always what your house is built on. Verse 28–29 is the crowd’s reaction. “When Jesus had finished saying these things, the crowds were amazed at his teaching, because he taught as one who had authority, and not as their teachers of the law.” Jesus didn’t just quote other rabbis. He spoke with the authority of the one these words came from in the first place. Series recap to weave in:
Lesson Passage The thread 1 · Up the Mountain Matt 5:1–12 Who God calls blessed 2 · Salt and Light Matt 5:13–16 Be flavor and illumination 3 · But I Say Matt 5:17–48 Heart, not just behavior 4 · When You Pray Matt 6:5–15 Talk to your Father 5 · Treasures and Two Masters Matt 6:19–24 What has your heart has your life 6 · Do Not Worry Matt 6:25–34 Trust the One who feeds sparrows 7 · Judge Not Matt 7:1–6 Check the plank before the speck 8 · Ask, Seek, Knock Matt 7:7–12 A good Father hears his kids 9 · House on the Rock Matt 7:24–29 Hear AND do. Build on Jesus. Jesus has been showing students a new way to be human. Tonight, he asks: are you going to build your life on it?
data.catch-their-attention--the-building-test:
Before the lesson: Have a Jenga tower built and ready on the table. (If you don’t have Jenga, stack books, Legos, or Solo cups.)
During the lesson: Gesture at the tower. Say:
This tower is solid. Nobody’s touching it. But — what would happen if I started pulling pieces from the bottom?
Pull a block from the bottom row. Then another. Keep going until it wobbles. Let a student knock it down if they want.
Then rebuild a tower and ask:
What if instead we pulled pieces from the top? Very different story, right?
What everything is built on matters more than what’s on top. Tonight — on our last night in the Sermon on the Mount — Jesus is going to tell us a story about exactly that.
data.center-stage#Center Stage:
What are the most important takeaways?
Jesus isn’t asking us to follow the rules harder — he’s inviting us to build our whole life on him. Hear AND do.
data.directing-the-scene#Directing the Scene: What learning activities might we do?
data.ponder-the-possibilities#Ponder the Poss:
Discuss together:
Of the eight earlier lessons, which one has been hardest for you to actually put into practice? What are some things middle schoolers try to build their lives on — sand that looks solid until the storm comes? Has a storm ever hit you and you realized you were building on sand? What happened? Jesus is super clear: hearing without doing is not enough. Is there something from these nine weeks you’ve heard but not actually done? What would it look like to build your middle-school life on Jesus? Not someday. This week.
data.prop-department#Prop Department:
What supplies do I need?
Bibles, one per student A Jenga tower (or Lego bricks, or a small stack of books) — for the building demo Whiteboard or large paper + markers Index cards — one per student Pens/pencils Christ light / candle + matches (for opening) Optional: a speaker + “New Way to Be Human” (Switchfoot) cued up for the close
data.read-and-interpret--matthew-72429#Read a:
Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:
We’ve been climbing this mountain for eight weeks. We heard who God calls blessed. We got called salt and light. We got pushed past the rules to the heart. We learned how to pray. We got warned about trying to serve two masters. We learned how to trust instead of worry. We got told to check our own plank before we go after anyone’s speck. We heard that our Father loves to be asked. Tonight Jesus wraps it all up. Listen for the moment the whole series comes to a point.
Read Matthew 7:24–29 together. Go around the circle. Read it slowly.
Discuss:
What’s the ONE difference between the wise builder and the foolish builder? — Both hear Jesus’ words. Only one puts them into practice. Do both houses get the storm? — Yes. Jesus doesn’t say if. He says when. What are the rains, streams, and winds in real life? — Middle school drama. Family stuff. Losses. Anxiety. The hard year. The heartbreak. The thing that blindsides you. Why does the wise builder’s house stand? — Because of what it’s built on. The foundation. The foolish builder isn’t evil. He’s not a pagan. He heard Jesus’ same words. What’s his problem? — He never did anything about them. He filed them away. Information without action. Look at verses 28–29. What’s the crowd’s reaction when Jesus finishes the whole sermon? — They’re amazed. Because Jesus doesn’t quote other teachers. He speaks like he’s the one the words come from. Say:
Here’s the punchline Jesus chose to close with. Not a bow. Not a blessing. A parable with teeth.
Everybody builds on something. Grades. Sports. Looks. Friends. Followers. Money. Achievement. Parents’ approval. Being funny. All of those things can be good — but not one of them can hold a life together when the storm rolls in.
And Jesus isn’t saying “listen to me, memorize me, share me, post about me.” He’s saying hear my words AND do them. Hearing without doing is sand. Hearing plus doing is rock.
That’s the whole series in one sentence: Jesus isn’t asking us to try harder at religion. He’s inviting us to build our whole life on him.
data.series-recap--eight-weeks-in-ninety-seco:
Before Ponder, flash back through the series fast on the whiteboard. Write each one up as you say it:
Lesson 1 — Up the Mountain. Jesus calls who blessed? Not who the world calls blessed. The poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek, the merciful, the peacemakers, the persecuted. Lesson 2 — Salt and Light. You ARE salt. You ARE light. Your life is supposed to flavor and illuminate the world. Lesson 3 — But I Say. Jesus doesn’t just want your behavior. He wants your heart. Lesson 4 — When You Pray. You have a Father. Talk to him like a kid. Short. Honest. Trusting. Lesson 5 — Treasures and Two Masters. Whatever has your heart has your life. You can’t serve God and money — pick one. Lesson 6 — Do Not Worry. Your Father feeds sparrows. He’s got you. Seek first his kingdom. Lesson 7 — Judge Not. Check your own plank before you go after anyone’s speck. Lesson 8 — Ask, Seek, Knock. Your Father loves to be asked. Treat people the way you want to be treated. Point back at the Jenga tower.
Every one of those isn’t a brick to balance on top. They’re the foundation. That’s what Jesus is telling us to build on.
data.start-with-prayer#Start with Prayer: Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. This is the last lesson of a nine-week series — ask God to help us not just finish the Sermon on the Mount, but build our lives on it.
data.turn-toward-action--what-are-you-buildin:
Hand each student an index card. Say:
Last Sermon on the Mount homework of the series. Three questions. Be honest.
What am I building my life on right now? (The honest answer. Not the Sunday-school answer.) What do I want to be building on? ONE thing I’ll do this week to lay a brick on Jesus. (Pray the Lord’s Prayer every morning. Reconcile with that person. Refuse to join that gossip chain. Hand that worry to God. Say hi to that kid nobody sits with.) Keep the card. Put it in your Bible, on your mirror, in your locker, in your phone case.
Close together. If you have the speaker cued up, play “New Way to Be Human” by Switchfoot as students finish writing. Pray together:
Jesus, we don’t want to build on sand. We’ve heard you for nine weeks. Help us do you for the next sixty years. Thank you for showing us a new way to be human. Make us wise builders. Make our lives stand on you. Amen.
Stand at the door as students leave. Say each student’s name and bless them out:
"[Name], you’ve been loved, you’ve belonged, and you’ve been made to make a difference. Now go build on the rock."
